metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Despite the success of approved vaccines in reducing SARS-CoV-2 transmission and mitigating the COVID-19 pandemic, significant challenges persist. mRNA vaccines proved highly effective and enabled rapid development, yet their stability continues to pose challenges for widespread distribution and accessibility. Additionally, the ongoing emergence and re-emergence of COVID-19 variants continue to threaten global health, placing considerable strain on healthcare systems and economies [ 59 ]. The short duration of the vaccine effectiveness, adverse effects, and challenges in achieving herd immunity have reduced overall coverage and efficacy [ 60 , 61 , 62 ]. Recent reports from 4 November 2024 indicate a resurgence of COVID-19 transmission across multiple countries, with confirmed cases exceeding 19,000 in the last month [ 63 ]. The continued emergence of new variants despite existing vaccines poses a major challenge to pandemic control and vaccination efforts [ 64 ]. The rapid evolution of SARS-CoV-2 from the prototype to Beta, Gamma, Delta, and Omicron variants has compromised vaccines’ effectiveness and led to breakthrough infections [ 65 , 66 ], highlighting the urgent need for booster doses that elicit cross-protective T-cells immunity [ 67 ]. Consequently, the durability of protection offered by current mRNA vaccines remains limited [ 68 ]. Together, these challenges highlight the need for a universal vaccine offering broad protection against evolving SARS-CoV-2 variants.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: The high mutation rates of SARS-CoV-2 underscore the urgent need to design effective universal vaccines capable of addressing current variants of concern and future emerging strains. Identifying and engineering potent immunogenic targets through innovative approaches is critical for developing universal COVID-19 vaccine platforms that provide broad protection against both existing and evolving viral variants. These approaches should focus on developing vaccines that provide broad protection against emerging variants and cross-protection against zoonotic coronaviruses [ 69 ]. Most of the current vaccines predominantly target the spike protein as the principal protective antigen, due to its critical role in viral entry [ 70 , 71 ]. An ideal approach to developing universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccine is to enhance protection and efficacy offered by already existing mRNA-based vaccines. A promising strategy through which this can be achieved is codon de-optimization, a technique previously applied to several viruses [ 72 , 73 ], resulting in viral attenuation, enhanced immunogenicity, and protection against challenge with parental strains. By optimizing mRNA sequence to increase stability, it can further improve vaccine efficacy and durability, while potentially boosting immunogenicity and broad reactivity, ultimately leading to robust neutralizing antibody responses against variants of concern [ 74 , 75 ]. Divergent mutations in the RBD and NTD domains of the spike protein, which are key targets for neutralizing antibodies, have driven the emergence of new variants [ 76 ]. Decoding these mutations offers a promising strategy for stabilizing vaccine antigens and enhancing their protective efficacy, potentially serving as a pivotal approach toward developing a universal vaccine. Moreover, extending stabilization to include the spike S2 subunits from SARS-CoV-2 as well as sarbecovirus such as SARS-CoV-1 (clade 1a) and PRD-0038 (clade 3) has conferred protection in mice against XBB.1.5 challenge [ 77 ]. Advanced computational tools are accelerating universal vaccine development. Machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI) will play a central role in this effort. AI pipelines are crucial for identifying conserved proteins and immunogenic T-cell epitopes across coronaviruses to enable cross-reactive immunity. Predictive models like EVE-Vax anticipate viral mutations, facilitating the design of vaccine antigens that strengthen defenses against emerging variants; integration of viral genomic structure with epidemiological data helps in forecasting high-risk mutations before they become widespread [ 78 , 79 ], which shifts our interaction with the virus pandemic from reactive responses to proactive pandemic prevention. In 2020, the Vaxign ML platform identified six proteins including the spike (S) protein and five non-structural proteins as highly protective antigens [ 80 ]; this strategy underscores the potential integration of this technology for leveraging antigens with broad antigenicity to develop universal vaccines while providing wide-spectrum protection against sarbecoviruses in general. Collectively, integrating optimized antigen design, conserved-target strategies, and AI-driven predictive tools offers a promising pathway toward universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccines capable of providing broad, durable protection against current and future coronavirus threats.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Developing a universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccine is vital for long-term disease control, offering broad protection against current and future variants while reducing the need for frequent boosters. The persistent evolution of SARS-CoV-2 and related coronaviruses constitutes a critical barrier to universal vaccine development. However, multi-subunit vaccines incorporating conserved spike RBD epitopes have demonstrated the capacity to elicit broadly neutralizing antibodies against multiple SARS-CoV-2 strains, including ancestral variants [ 81 ]. Furthermore, nanoparticle-based polyvalent protein cocktails exhibit potential for cross-protection against SARS-CoV-2-like viruses’ clade 1 sarbecoviruses [ 82 ] Nanoparticles displaying a mosaic of SARS-CoV-2 and animal betacoronavirus RBDs elicited antibodies with enhanced cross-reactivity against diverse RBDs, effectively neutralizing heterologous pseudotyped coronaviruses [ 39 , 83 ]. Additionally, Helicobacter pylori -assembled conserved epitopes from prior SARS-CoV-2 variants elicited durable and robust humoral and cellular immune responses [ 84 ]. As a result, leveraging multi-subunit vaccines targeting conserved RBDs and mosaic nanoparticle formulation represents a promising approach to broad-spectrum immunity and universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccine development, with potential cover emerging and re-emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants and related coronavirus.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Furthermore, the discovery of broadly cross-reactive monoclonal antibodies against animal sarbecoviruses, SARS-CoV-1, and SARS-CoV-2 in plasma of COVID-19 convalescents and vaccine recipients underscores a strong foundation for universal vaccine development [ 85 ]. Combining ancestral SARS-CoV-2 antigens with broadly neutralizing anti-sarbecovirus antibodies offers a promising strategy for universal vaccine design to prevent future SARS-CoV-2 and related sarbecovirus outbreaks.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: This exploration of universal vaccine candidates moves the anticipation from chasing variants to a proactive strategy of anticipating viral evolution. On one hand, strategies like multi-subunit and mosaic-nanoparticle vaccines are essentially “teaching” the immune system to recognize the common architectural features shared by different coronaviruses. On the other, the identification of broadly neutralizing antibodies in convalescent individuals acts as a vital proof-of-concept, demonstrating that a universal response is not merely a theoretical construct but a tangible biological outcome that can be elicited and engineered. Therefore, we can predict a paradigm shift in vaccinology, where the strategic goal is no longer just to end the current pandemic but to establish a foundational, cross-protective immunity against an entire class of viral threats.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: The S2 subunit is highly conserved across SARS-CoV-2 variants and subvariants, as well as other betacoronaviruses, making it a promising universal vaccine antigen for broad protection against emerging variants. Broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) targeting the S2 subunit, isolated from vaccinated donors, confer protection against the three major betacoronaviruses SARS-CoV-1, SARS-CoV-2, and MERS-CoV that have emerged over the past two decades [ 86 ]. Notably, one bnAb epitope was highly conserved across most variants and retained conformational stability in both the pre- and post-fusion states of the spike protein [ 87 ]. These findings highlight the S2 subunit as a key target for universal coronavirus vaccine development. Immunization of two mouse models with a prefusion-stabilized SARS-CoV-2 S2 subunit, achieved via interprotomer disulfide bonds, elicited broadly neutralizing responses against multiple sarbecoviruses and protected against lethal challenge [ 88 ]. Moreover, combining two conserved sarbecovirus proteins, the S2 subunit and the nucleocapsid (N) protein successfully elicited broad systemic neutralizing antibody responses and robust Th1 immunity, as evidenced by IFN-γ secretion [ 89 ]. This synergy reveals the potential of the S2 subunit as a central component of universal coronavirus vaccine strategy and development. Owing to its high sequence conservation, structural stability across pre- and post-fusion states, and ability to induce broadly neutralizing and cellular immune responses, the S2 subunit represents a promising antigen for achieving durable, cross-protective immunity against current and emerging coronaviruses. Therefore, the focus on the S2 subunit represents a strategic move in SARS-CoV-2 vaccine design, deliberately moving away from the hyper-variable S1/RBD region that drives immune escape.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Another promising approach is targeting conserved non-spike antigens, as studies have suggested these highly conserved SARS-CoV-2 proteins can confer robust, broad cross-protective immunity against multiple variants of concern (VOCs) [ 90 , 91 , 92 , 93 ]. Ten conserved antigens have been identified, seven of which are preferentially recognized by both CD4 + and CD8 + T cells in unimmunized patients [ 90 ]. Furthermore, the combining of highly conserved antigens that target both B-cell and T-cell epitopes alongside with the Spike protein elicited cross-protective immunity, accelerated viral clearance, and mitigated lung pathology from highly pathogenic variants [ 90 , 91 ]. Moreover, a multi-epitope vaccine incorporating S1-RBD-sFc and conserved Th and CTL peptides from Sarbecovirus N, M, and S2 proteins induced a durable Th1-biased response characterized by IFN-γ production and robust cytotoxic CD8+ T-cell activity [ 92 ]. Conserved antigens from other proteins, such as the SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid (N) protein, a key factor in viral replication and pathogenesis [ 94 ], may serve as an optimal target for this approach, offering broader functionality [ 95 ]. Inclusion of the nucleocapsid (N) protein in vaccine design broadens the immune response by eliciting both humoral and cellular immunity, potentially providing longer-lasting protection against SARS-CoV-2 variants in hamsters [ 96 , 97 ]. Moreover, antibodies against nucleocapsid proteins have been detected in both unvaccinated and vaccinated individuals [ 98 , 99 ]. Additionally, the use of a highly conserved SARS-CoV-2 ORF3a ectodomain can elicit robust systemic anti-ORF3a antibody response and induced T-cell immune response in the lung [ 100 ]. Figure 2 illustrate the anticipated current and future directions of SARS-CoV-2 universal vaccine platforms. Although early studies suggested conserved proteins have low immunogenicity, the nucleocapsid (N), ORF3a ectodomain protein, and conserved domains within the spike protein remain strong candidates for next-generation universal COVID-19 vaccines, with the potential to induce both cellular and humoral immune responses.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Although current vaccines have been highly effective in mitigating severe SARS-CoV-2 infections and mortality, the emergence of variants of concern (VOCs) and variants of interest (VOIs), coupled with their immune escape capabilities, underscores the need for ongoing development of enhanced vaccine strategies. One of these strategies involves identifying novel antigenic epitopes that can confer a strong and broad immune response. Utilizing a multivalent approach that incorporates multiple antigenic epitopes has shown promise in discovering new targets. For example, in BALB/c mice, a multivalent mRNA vaccine elicits broad neutralizing antibody responses against SARS-CoV-2 VOCs [ 101 ]. The high degree of conservation and structural stability positions of the S2 subunit marks a strategic starting point for identifying novel spike antigenic epitopes. S2-specific monoclonal antibodies from recovered human individuals bind conserved epitopes across multiple S2 regions and broadly neutralize SARS-CoV-2 variants, SARS-CoV-1 and related zoonotic sarbecoviruses [ 102 ], as well as other clade 1 sarbecoviruses [ 103 ].

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: In a recent study, Wang et al. engineered RBD sequences with six mutations and integrated eight RBDs from zoonotic sarbecoviruses into a single vaccine design, successfully induced cross-reactive immune responses in a murine model [ 81 ]. A trivalent RBD scaffold protein has demonstrated durable protection and cross-neutralization against JN.1 and XBB lineage [ 104 , 105 ]. Consequently, the conserved S2 subunit and mosaic RBD platforms represent potential antigenic targets for a next-generation universal vaccine, offering broad protection against diverse sarbecoviruses and emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: This body of research outlines a sophisticated, dual-pronged strategy for universal vaccine development, moving beyond the limitations of single-antigen platforms. The first approach involves targeting the highly conserved S2 subunit, essentially attacking the virus’s stable architectural core to elicit a deep and durable immune response. The second, complementary approach uses multivalent or mosaic-RBD platforms, which aims to achieve breadth by preemptively exposing the immune system to a diverse array of potential future mutational pathways. Together, these convergent strategies demonstrate that the path forward lies not in finding a single perfect epitope, but in intelligently combining conserved and variable antigens to generate a robust immune response towards the virus.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Following the initial rollout of mRNA-based SARS-CoV-2 vaccines, research shifted toward broadly protective platforms for universal vaccine development, driven by the emergence of variants of concern (VOCs). In August 2022, the first universal SARS-CoV-2 DNA vaccine combined polyvalent RBD with the membrane and nucleoproteins, inducing cross-reactive antibodies (Wuhan-1), Beta, Delta, and Omicron variants and priming nucleoprotein-specific T cells [ 106 ]. Another multi-subunit universal vaccine provided protection against ancestral SARS-CoV-2 and Omicron subvariants (BA.1, BA.2, BA.2.75, BA.4.6, BA.5), inhibiting Omicron replication, and fully protecting mice from lethal SARS-CoV-2 Delta and SARS-CoV challenges [ 81 ]. Both vaccines show promise as universal COVID-19 and pan-sarbecovirus candidates, capable of preventing current and future SARS-CoV-2 variant and SARS-related outbreaks. An immunoinformatic-designed-epitope vaccine combining SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid with M2e and HA2 influenza proteins shows potential as a universal candidate [ 107 ]. Furthermore, more recently the U.S. department of Health and Human Services (HHS) announced a next-generation universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccine using a beta-propiolactone-inactivated whole-virus approach [ 108 ]. Table 2 : Summary of ongoing and recent trials investigating universal vaccine platforms. Current universal vaccine efforts focus on multi-epitope designs, conserved antigens, and inclusion of antigens from other coronaviruses and sarbecoviruses to achieve broad protection against emerging variants and coronaviruses. However, this goal remains unmet to date. Indeed, the current landscape of universal vaccine development marks a clear transformation from the initial, singular focus on the spike protein. This new phase is characterized by a multi-pronged approach, experimenting with diverse platforms—from DNA to inactivated whole-virus—and combining various antigens, including conserved non-spike proteins and epitopes from multiple variants. While a definitive solution remains elusive, this collective effort underscores a consensus that achieving broad, durable protection requires abandoning simple, single-antigen designs in favor of these more complex, multi-faceted strategies.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Despite the rapid deployment of COVID-19 vaccines, the ongoing viral evolution and emergence of VOCs highlight the urgent need for universal vaccine strategies capable of providing broad and durable protection. Approaches targeting conserved viral epitopes that elicit robust cell-mediated immunity and induce broadly neutralizing antibodies are essential for developing universal vaccines that offer durable protection against SARS-CoV-2 variants as well as a broad range of sarbecoviruses [ 85 , 111 ] and potential future zoonotic threats. The emergence of novel variants increases the need for pan-coronavirus protection, durable immune responses, and optimized antigen delivery platforms, all of which remain major challenges in next-generation COVID-19 vaccine development [ 112 ]. Such challenges necessitate utilizing new tools, including artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) for antigen selection and vaccine design in order to facilitate the development of universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccines. These technologies can streamline antigen selection and immunogen design by predicting antigenic epitopes, assessing immunogenicity, and designing simulation models to enhance immunogen stability and coverage [ 113 ]. A notable example is Dense Convolutional Network (DenseNet), a platform developed by Chinese researchers to predict protein–ligand interactions [ 114 ], which is critical for designing a universal vaccine by assessing receptor-binding capability. Figure 3 illustrates an overview of current SARS-CoV-2 vaccine platforms and their future potential for the development of universal vaccines. Integrating one or more of these approaches will be instrumental in leveraging advanced technologies to develop novel universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccines capable of combating this highly disruptive pathogen.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Universal vaccines have long been envisioned as a solution to rapidly evolving viral pathogens including influenza and human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) [ 115 ]. Yet, their successful development remains constrained by multiple scientific, technological, and translational challenges. In the context of SARS-CoV-2, these challenges arise from the interplay between viral evolution, vaccine design limitations, and practical considerations related to safety, manufacturing, and implementation [ 116 , 117 ]. A primary scientific obstacle in universal vaccine development is the selection of an optimal vaccine target. Viral regions that remain conserved across variants serve as ideal vaccine targets because they are less likely to mutate and are therefore more likely to confer broad protection against both circulating and future variants [ 115 , 118 ]. However, these conserved regions are often inadequately immunogenic and rarely induce strong neutralizing antibody responses [ 115 ]. Conversely, highly immunogenic viral epitopes include those located within the spike protein receptor-binding domain (RBD) and N-terminal domain (NTD) [ 23 ]. Unfortunately, these are subject to intense immune selection pressure and consequently accumulate mutations rapidly [ 23 , 116 ]. Vaccines targeting these regions can provide strong protection initially but become vulnerable to immune escape as variants emerge. This trade-off between conservation and immunogenicity complicates the design of vaccines capable of inducing both potent and durable cross-variant immunity.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Ongoing viral evolution further complicates universal vaccine development. SARS-CoV-2 continues to diversify globally, generating variants with enhanced transmissibility or immune escape properties [ 119 , 120 ]. While advances in artificial intelligence and computational modeling allow prediction of potential mutation patterns, incorporating all predicted antigenic changes into a single vaccine is not feasible [ 121 ]. Excessively multivalent vaccine formulations would increase manufacturing complexity and regulatory challenges while also risking increased reactogenicity [ 122 ]. Highly complex antigen compositions may provoke stronger inflammatory responses or adverse reactions, potentially undermining vaccine safety and acceptance. Thus, even when future variants can be anticipated, practical immunological and safety limits restrict how much antigenic diversity can realistically be incorporated into a universal vaccine platform.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Beyond biological challenges, translational and economic considerations play a major role. Universal vaccines would require extensive preclinical studies and large-scale clinical trials to demonstrate protection across variants and populations, increasing both development time and cost [ 123 ]. Manufacturing processes must also remain adaptable to future viral evolution while maintaining scalability and affordability, particularly for global vaccination campaigns. Regulatory pathways for vaccines designed to anticipate future variants are still evolving, further complicating deployment timelines.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Collectively, these scientific and practical challenges explain the lack of a universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccine even after years of extensive funding and research. This remains true not only for SARS-CoV-2 but also for other viruses including influenza, which emerged long before and thus has certainly had significantly more funding and research. In order to pave the way for a universal vaccine, advances in antigen design and predictive modeling are crucial. Equally important are improved understanding of immune responses, scalable manufacturing strategies, and regulatory frameworks capable of supporting the development and deployment of universally protective vaccine platforms against current and future coronavirus variants.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Ongoing SARS-CoV-2 evolution has revealed the inherent limitations of spike-focused, variant-matched vaccines, which struggle to maintain durable and cross-variant protection. While current vaccines continue to reduce infection rates and mitigate severity of disease, a key factor driving the urgency for a universal vaccine is the significant genetic and antigenic evolution of the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, as well as diminished protection provided by spike-based vaccines.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: Universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccines offer a promising strategy to curb the emergence and re-emergence of variants by targeting conserved regions of the spike protein alone or combined with other stable elements such as S2 and leveraging multivalent platforms incorporating epitopes from related coronaviruses. These approaches aim to induce broad neutralizing antibodies and, critically, robust and durable T-cell responses. Since T-cell immunity is less affected by antigenic variation, enhancing cross-reactive cellular immunity may provide more resilient and long-lasting protection.

metadata.title: The Feasibility of Developing a Universal SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine.

text: In summary, a universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccine platform that integrates multivalent and conserved targets with strong t-cell immunogenicity is vital for the feasibility of a universal SARS-CoV-2 vaccine for long-term preparedness against future coronavirus threats.

text: In the aftermath of more than an estimated 1,230,000 cumulative deaths in the United States (through August 15, 2025) directly attributable to COVID-19, 1 representing the greatest global pandemic since the Great Influenza of 1918, public health measures taken to protect the public, including masking, social distancing, remote schooling, and lockdowns, have become the target of sustained public debate. The development and broad use of COVID-19 vaccines, with mandated use in some cases, have drawn particular scrutiny, with some questioning the safety of vaccines that were developed, evaluated, and authorized rapidly and introduced at a nearly unprecedented scale. This paper reviews the evolution of our understanding of COVID-19 vaccine safety, particularly focused on the Moderna mRNA COVID-19 vaccine (also known as mRNA-1273). Measures taken to monitor its safety profile during development through Emergency Use Authorization (December 2020) in the US are described, including post-authorization safety work to identify, evaluate, and address safety concerns. Where appropriate, other vaccines are mentioned, as many studies pooled data from the Moderna and Pfizer-BioNTech mRNA COVID-19 vaccines for analysis. The manuscript primarily focuses upon US data, not only to manage the scope of a lengthy manuscript, but also because mRNA-1273 has been extensively used in the US and the infrastructure for monitoring vaccine safety was considered especially robust. Meetings of the Advisory Committee for Immunization Practices (ACIP), which is coordinated by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and provides recommendations for vaccine use, documented the safety topics considered the most relevant for public health.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Although the precise origin of SARS-CoV-2 remains disputed, it is clear that the first cluster of pneumonia cases of unknown etiology was publicly reported from Western China in late 2019. Within 1 week, these cases were attributed to a novel coronavirus, following isolation of the virus from an infected patient. 2 Within 3 weeks of the first identified Chinese case, a traveler from Wuhan became the first reported US case of SARS-CoV-2 infection. 3 On January 31, 2020, the US declared a public health emergency, 4 citing the novelty of the virus, absence of prior human immunity, disease severity, evidence of efficient and sustained person-to-person transmission, and concerns raised by other recent novel coronavirus outbreaks, including Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV; 37% case fatality rate [CFR]) 5 and severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus (SARS-CoV; >8000 cases and 10% CFR). 6 The US decision paralleled the World Health Organization (WHO) declaration of COVID-19, caused by SARS-CoV-2, as a public health emergency of international concern. 7

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: By 2020, it had become apparent that the virus was highly transmissible and associated with CFRs as high as 14.9% in high-risk groups (e.g. older adults, those with comorbidities). 8 The rapid increase in severe cases requiring intensive medical treatment also raised concern about the capacity of the US healthcare system to prevent disease transmission and health care system overload (“flattening the curves”). 9 , 10 During this period, treatment options were limited and mostly empirical. In the absence of safe and efficacious treatments to treat COVID-19, 11 development of a preventive vaccine quickly emerged as an urgent global priority. By October 2020, nearly 200 candidate vaccines were in development, employing a wide range of platforms, from novel to well-established, and human studies were underway for more than 40 different candidate vaccines. 12

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: In May 2020, in response to the public health impact of the COVID-19 pandemic and its effect on the US economy, the federal government launched Operation Warp Speed (OWS), an initiative to accelerate development, evaluation, and manufacturing of COVID-19 vaccines. 13 OWS provided funding and operational support for vaccine development with the goal of supporting authorization and large scale distribution within an unprecedented time frame (>300 million doses by January 2021). OWS was designed to accelerate development by integrating the respective strengths of the federal, academic, and industry sectors. Along with AstraZeneca, Janssen, Sanofi/GSK, and Novavax, Moderna was one of five recipients of OWS funding for research and development. 14

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Despite substantial uncertainties, vaccine development under OWS proceeded rapidly. Early in the pandemic, fundamental questions remained regarding SARS-CoV-2 virology and vaccine development, including antigen selection, the potential for viral mutation and escape, correlates of protection, and the durability of natural immunity. There were theoretical safety concerns, including potential for vaccine-enhanced disease, i.e. the possibility that vaccination in persons previously infected with SARS-CoV-2 might result in severe COVID-19). Nevertheless, OWS enabled the development and full safety and efficacy evaluation of two candidate vaccines from preclinical research to Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) 15 within 9 months, compared with the typical vaccine timeline to formal licensure of 5–10 y, while maintaining appropriate regulatory and clinical trial oversight mechanisms.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: OWS achieved its objective through coordinated federal, academic, and industry efforts within a regulatory framework that incorporated transparent and rigorous clinical trial safety oversight. This framework also integrated other complementary federal efforts (harmonization of clinical trial protocols, coordination of research sites [permitting multiple large-scale trials to proceed noncompetitively] and concurrent evaluation of multiple vaccine candidates under a centralized data and safety monitoring board [DSMB]). 16 , 17 The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) utilized the existing EUA framework while issuing specific COVID-19 vaccine guidance and providing regular opportunities to interact with sponsors to address emerging scientific and regulatory questions. 15 , 18 The trials also included protocol-specified safety assessments and ongoing DSMB review of accumulating safety and efficacy data. Pivotal phase III trials adhered to contemporary safety standards, including a requirement that at least 3,000 vaccine participants would need to be followed up for a median of at least 60 d after their final dose. The follow-up period was selected based on the historical observation that most immune-mediated vaccine adverse events (AEs) typically occur within this period. 19 The FDA was steadfast in adhering to its regulatory requirements, maintaining them despite external pressure to relax standards to expedite vaccine approval. 20 Ongoing safety and efficacy data were reviewed frequently by the DSMB, often at biweekly intervals. 21 , 22

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Additional factors supporting efficient vaccine development included rolling regulatory reviews and some temporal overlap of clinical studies, whereby successive clinical studies were initiated before the formal completion of earlier phase studies, accomplishing this without compromising data quality (e.g. before all the administrative details of a preceding study had been completed and submitted). Finally, OWS accelerated availability of commercial quantities of vaccine by assuming the financial risk of funding manufacturing capacity much earlier than usual, again doing so under strict quality oversight. The government also funded construction of dedicated manufacturing sites, well before manufacturers would normally commit resources for such investment. 23

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Development, clinical evaluation, and safety oversight of mRNA-1273 through Emergency Use Authorization. The genetic sequence of SARS-CoV-2 was shared by Chinese researchers on January 11, 2020. Compared with strategies for development of vaccines that were licensed at that time, mRNA technology offers certain advantages for rapid antigen design. 24 Based on prior collaboration developing a MERS-CoV mRNA vaccine, the National Institutes of Health and Moderna were able to finalize antigen design within 2 d following publication of the SARS-CoV-2 genome. 25 , 26 A decade of experience optimizing the mRNA platform and manufacturing processes for use in nine investigational vaccines permitted rapid preparation of clinically representative material for the COVID-19 candidate vaccine (mRNA-1273), an mRNA-LNP vaccine encoding a prefusion-stabilized spike protein ( Figure 1 ). The prototype vaccine was then tested in nonclinical models to assess immunogenicity, protective efficacy (through a challenge study), toxicity, biodistribution kinetics, and to consider the potential for vaccine-enhanced disease (which had been raised as a possible safety concern for all COVID-19 vaccines). 27 , 28

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Based upon initial data accruing from the nonclinical models, first-in-human studies began in March 2020 in accordance with existing FDA guidance. 22 , 29 To accelerate efficient vaccine development, the FDA provided flexibility in conducting nonclinical studies, based on sufficient established knowledge (safety, toxicology, biodistribution, developmental and reproductive toxicology [DART], immunogenicity, and animal challenge) about the respective platform-based technology. 30 Thus, the timing of the initiation of clinical studies was not as strictly tied to completion of nonclinical studies and certain studies were conducted in parallel. The phase I dose-ranging safety and immunogenicity study of the Moderna investigational vaccine, mRNA-1273, evaluated two intramuscular doses of 25, 100, or 250 µg administered 28 d apart (15 participants/group). At 57 d following the first dose (i.e. 4 weeks after the second dose), the most common solicited AEs were chills, headache, myalgia, and injection site pain. Systemic AEs occurred more commonly after the second dose, especially at the highest dose. 31 No serious AEs (SAEs) were identified through 6 months of follow-up. 32 This cohort was followed up and as of 119 d following dosing, no new AEs considered by the investigator to be attributable to vaccine exposure had occurred, nor had any SAEs occurred. 33

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Based on phase I study findings, the phase II study (initiated May 2020) evaluated the safety, reactogenicity, and immunogenicity of 50 µg vs 100 µg vs placebo in 600 participants, stratified by two age cohorts (aged 18 to <55 and ≥55 y), who were randomly assigned in an observer-blinded manner to a two-dose regimen, 28 d apart. 34 The safety findings of this study (follow-up 4 weeks after the second dose) were similar to those of the phase I results: local and systemic reactogenicity were common and generally dose related; they were typically of short (up to 2–3 d) duration; and grade 3 reactions (i.e. typically preventing daily activities and often requiring medical intervention) were rare. 34

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Immunogenicity results together with these safety findings 31 , 35 led to the initiation of the randomized, observer-blinded, placebo-controlled phase 3 pivotal trial on July 27, 2020, which evaluated two doses of mRNA-1273 100 µg administered 28 d apart. 36 Participants were age-stratified (aged 18 to <65, ≥65 y) and included persons with underlying comorbidities (to support generalizability). Solicited AEs were assessed through 7 d after each dose, unsolicited AEs for 28 d, SAEs and AEs of special interest (AESIs) for up to 24 months. In accordance with FDA requirements, the trial enrolled at least 30,000 participants, which is typical for a phase III vaccine trial, enabling a thorough evaluation of both efficacy and safety. The primary endpoint was >50% efficacy against virologically confirmed, symptomatic COVID-19 occurring at least 14 d following dose 2 (with lower bound of the two-sided 95% confidence interval [CI] >30%). Per FDA requirements for safety monitoring, a minimum of 3,000 vaccine participants were to be followed for a median of at least 60 d after their final dose. The FDA Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee (VRBPAC) EUA review (December 17, 2020) evaluated the safety data set (vaccine recipients who received at least one dose) collected through November 25, 2020, by which time 53.8% of the 15,184 (8,170) participants who received dose 2 of the vaccine had been followed for at least 60 d. 37

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: On December 17, 2020, the VRBPAC reviewed the EUA submission package for mRNA-1273, including the phase 3 study (Coronavirus Efficacy study, AKA COVE), which demonstrated 94.1% efficacy (95% CI: 89.3%–96.8%) among >30,000 participants representative of the general population in preventing symptomatic COVID-19 illness (defined as new onset of clinical symptoms consistent with COVID-19 plus laboratory confirmation of SARS-CoV-2 infection) with onset starting 14 d or more after the second dose, occurring in persons without prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. One hundred percent efficacy was demonstrated against the secondary endpoint of severe disease (defined as cases of symptomatic COVID-19 illness [as above] plus adjudicated evidence of clinical signs of severe systemic illness [defined as any of the following findings: ≥30 breaths/minute respiratory rate; ≥125 beats/minute heart rate; SpO 2 ≤ 93% on room air at sea level; PaO 2 /FiO 2 <300 mmHg; respiratory failure or acute respiratory distress syndrome; evidence of shock; significant acute renal, hepatic, or neurologic dysfunction; admission to an intensive care unit; or death). 36 Vaccine tolerability was comparable across groups. Solicited AEs (e.g. injection site pain) and systemic AEs were again common, mostly mild-to-moderate, and resolving within a few days. No imbalance in SAEs or deaths was observed. There were no concerns highlighted for subgroups, including age, sex, race, ethnicity, comorbidities, or prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. VRBPAC noted that special populations (those who were pregnant or breastfeeding) had been excluded from enrollment, that no cases of severe hypersensitivity had been reported, and that longer term safety follow-up would be informative. 38 These issues were to be further evaluated by clinical research and by routine and enhanced pharmacovigilance (e.g. post-authorization safety studies). 39 Safety data for phase III trial participants were to be followed up for a total of 24 months, including outcomes of incidental pregnancies. Comprehensive monthly safety reports were to be provided to the FDA, summarizing accruing data on AEs, including by subpopulations of interest. Additionally, Moderna agreed to implement a registry to evaluate pregnancy outcomes and to conduct an active surveillance study of 23 prespecified AESIs.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: The EUA approvals of mRNA-1273 (Moderna, December 18, 2020), BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech, December 11, 2020), and Ad26.COV2.S (Janssen, February 27, 2021) were followed by the most intensive safety monitoring in US history. 40

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: The Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, which typically meets thrice annually, convened 31 times between June 2020 and August 2022 to prepare for and monitor COVID-19 vaccines. 41 Anticipating the introduction of COVID- 19 vaccines, early meetings focused on clinical assessment (safety monitoring, potential risks), prioritization, and resource allocation of limited vaccine supplies. Following their introduction, subsequent meetings reviewed emergent vaccine effectiveness and safety data, benefit-risk, and performance in special populations (e.g. immune-compromised), updating their assessment as the virus evolved and the vaccines were deployed on a massive scale. Post-authorization safety evaluation for the COVID-19 vaccines, including mRNA-1273, relied upon multiple complementary data sources and systems to identify and assess both expected reactions as well as potential rare safety signals as vaccine use expanded.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Pharmacovigilance requires a tightly regulated set of activities intended to detect, evaluate, and prevent (or mitigate) untoward effects of a vaccine (or drug) aiming to ensure that those products with a favorable benefit/risk profile are used in a way that maximizes benefits while minimizing risks. Manufacturers systematically and regularly review a range of data sources, including preclinical, clinical, and observational research studies; published literature; social media; and spontaneous reports to identify safety signals (i.e. information suggesting a treatment may cause an effect, either beneficial or harmful, that merits further evaluation and corroboration). 42 In practice, only a small proportion of spontaneous reports or new safety signals observed following licensure are both confirmed and found to materially affect benefit/risk. Most are rejected because there are more likely explanations for the finding, basic information is missing, or multiple reports are received for the same case. 43 In the case of COVID-19 vaccines, safety monitoring was facilitated by publication of a set of 18 AESIs that specified particular AEs considered relevant for COVID-19 vaccines. 44 These AESIs provided a framework for more intensive monitoring authorized COVID-19 vaccines, including mRNA-1273 ( Table 1 ).

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: By the end of December 2021, more than 500 million doses of COVID-19 vaccines are estimated to have been administered in the US, of which approximately 40% were mRNA-1273. 44 In comparison, the total number of COVID-19 vaccine doses administered dwarfed by more than 3:1 the number of influenza vaccine doses (the most widely used vaccine prior to COVID-19) administered in the prior year. 50 Safety monitoring was strengthened through new systems such as V-Safe, a smart phone–based active surveillance tool in which vaccine recipients voluntarily and self-reported their vaccine experiences. Developed by the CDC, V-safe was used to provide real-time insight into health events following vaccination, and separately to follow maternal complications, pregnancy outcomes, and early infant health among people who were pregnant at the time of vaccination. 40 Existing systems, such as the FDA Biologics Effectiveness and Safety (BEST) Initiative, were adapted for near real-time evaluation of safety signals using both Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) and commercial claims data following more than 225 million persons, and the FDA-CMS Medicare 4 data systems were newly deployed to review daily Medicare claims for vaccine safety. 51 These activities augmented long-standing core elements of US vaccine safety surveillance, including the Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS, which is the national passive spontaneous reporting system and jointly maintained by the FDA and CDC) and the Vaccine Safety Datalink (VSD), an active surveillance activity that collects data from approximately 12 million persons (permitting nearly real-time identification of safety signals through rapid cycle analysis, as well as their evaluation through formal observational research studies). 52 Among other attributes, the VSD is informative because it provides background rates for AEs, which is one of several important criteria used to evaluate causality. 53 Collectively, these resources provided a greater range of data to inform safety in a timely manner. V-Safe was especially useful in describing reactogenicity, other short-term reactions, and pregnancy follow up. VAERS served as a national early warning system for rare or unexpected events after mRNA-1273 vaccination. FDA BEST and CMS data supported near real-time evaluation of potential signals in very large populations, including older adults. The VSD provided prompt signal detection, background rate comparisons, and the infrastructure to conduct formal observational analyses to refine safety signals and assess whether they were causally related and of concern.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: At the time of EUA, the product safety information reflected data accrued during product development, including the risk for solicited local and systemic reactions (collectively referred to as reactogenicity, i.e. expected short-lived local and systemic signs and symptoms, such as injection site pain or headache occurring shortly after immunization, and that reflect an inflammatory response primarily driven by an innate immune response). 54 , 55 Additionally, this information included a contraindication for persons with a known history of a severe allergic reactions (such as anaphylaxis) to any known component of the vaccine. Post-authorization surveillance extended safety evaluation beyond clinical trial findings by assessing the vaccine in broader populations and under real-world conditions.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Since December 2020 EUA, thousands of safety topics have been evaluated for mRNA-1273, from the common to the rare and unexpected. Safety signals generally required evaluation across different systems and included clinical and epidemiologic review before conclusions could be drawn regarding causality and their relevance to benefit-risk.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: To structure this review, safety topics are arranged sequentially by category. Discussed first are those AEs that have been determined to represent clinically important safety risks (through clinical trials and/or post-authorization surveillance) and that have warranted risk communication and mitigation (e.g. through product labeling). These are followed by review of findings for special populations, which are defined as groups for which vaccine safety, immunogenicity, and effectiveness may differ from the general population due to biological, developmental, physiological, or clinical factors, and for whom safety data were lacking during development or at the time of EUA. A discussion of AESIs follows. AESIs are pre-specified safety topics identified prior to EUA (or subsequently) that are of interest because of their potential causal association with a vaccine, based on biological plausibility, prior experience with a similar vaccine or vaccine platform, or known pathophysiology, and merit focused monitoring, and evaluation. Finally, other emergent safety topics are reviewed.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: The EUA contained a contraindication for use in those with known history of a severe allergic reaction to any vaccine component; however, no case of anaphylaxis had occurred in the clinical development program. Within weeks of their introduction, reports of anaphylaxis among recipients of mRNA-1273 were received by VAERS, with the CDC reporting 10 cases (a rate of 2.5 cases per million doses administered). Of these, nine occurred in persons with documented history of allergies/allergic reactions, of whom five had a prior history of anaphylaxis. 56 These rates are comparable to those for anaphylaxis following influenza immunization 57 and far lower than those reported for all-cause anaphylaxis in the general population. 58 Within weeks, the CDC issued recommendations to monitor for and manage allergic reactions following receipt of COVID-19 vaccines. 59

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Viral infections are considered the most frequent causes of both myocarditis and pericarditis. 60 Myocarditis refers to inflammation of myocardial tissue, most commonly following viral infection, with clinical findings ranging from mild self-limited disease to severe cardiac impairment. Pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardial sac surrounding the heart, also often triggered by viral infection. Cases are typically mild and self-limited but may be associated with severe complications, such as pericardial effusion, tamponade, or pericardial fibrosis. Myopericarditis shares features of each syndrome. Diagnosis and management vary depending upon the clinical presentation. 61 , 62

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Prior to the emergence of the COVID-19 pandemic, myocarditis had been observed in persons infected with either of two closely related coronaviruses, SARS-CoV and MERS-CoV. 44 , 63–68 Within months of the first reports of SARS-CoV-2 it was recognized as a complication of COVID-19, 66–68 and myocarditis was found to be up to 16 times more frequent in hospitalized patients with COVID-19 compared with those without COVID-19, with the greatest risk among those aged <16 y or over 75 y. 69

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Bmyopericarditis had also been observed following the introduction (in 2002) of smallpox vaccine (modified vaccinia virus Ankara, live non-replicating) in US military personnel, 65 which prompted the Safety Platform for Emergency vACcines (SPEAC) to list it as an AESI for investigational COVID-19 vaccines. 44

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: In clinical development leading up to the December 2020 EUA for mRNA-1273 there had been no associated reported cases of myocarditis or pericarditis; however, in April 2021, several myocarditis cases in BNT162b2 vaccine recipients were reported to be under investigation in Israel. 70 Nearly contemporaneously, the US Department of Defense reported suspected cases occurring in recipients of either mRNA vaccine. 71 Initial review of other data sources during these early months after EUA, including Kaiser-Permanente Northern California (December 14, 2020–June 26, 2021) did not detect a significant risk for myocarditis in association with the mRNA vaccines. 52

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: These reports immediately prompted intense investigation of post-vaccination myocarditis and pericarditis by regulators, public health authorities, and the biopharmaceutical companies, 72 , 73 to determine whether the vaccines were causally associated with the outcomes (including whether they were occurring at rates above background rates in the vaccinated population). During the period leading up to a revised EUA Fact Sheet (June 24, 2021) that described the risk of myocarditis and pericarditis for mRNA-1273, 74 it became clear that the cases followed a common pattern: risk was greatest for younger males, typically occurring within a few days following vaccination, most commonly after the second dose of the primary series, and that most cases resolved within a few days with conservative management (e.g. rest, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents). 72 , 75 The observations were central to and included in regulatory risk communication as well as ongoing safety monitoring.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: In July 2021, the CDC published working case definitions of myocarditis, pericarditis, and myopericarditis. 76 At that time, they estimated that for the group at highest risk (males aged 12–17 y shortly following a second vaccine dose), for every million doses of either COVID-19 mRNA vaccine received, approximately 56–69 cases of myocarditis would be expected to occur. This estimate was counterbalanced by the estimated benefit of preventing 5,700 hospitalizations, 71 intensive care unit (ICU) admissions, and 2 deaths. Data from the National Patient-Centered Research Network (PCORnet) indicated that the risk for myocarditis/pericarditis attributable to COVID-19 disease significantly exceeded that of COVID-19 vaccine – associated myocarditis in all age and sex groups. 77 A systematic review comparing the clinical features of COVID-19–associated myocarditis with those associated with mRNA COVID-19 vaccines found substantive differences, with cases due to SARS-CoV-2 infection more likely to require ICU admission, to require the use of inotropic agents or beta-blockers to support blood pressure and cardiac output, or to result in complications or death. 78

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Myocarditis/pericarditis continues to be carefully monitored. Rates reported through the FDA BEST network (which pooled results for mRNA-1273 and BNT162b2) have declined over time. For the period 2020–2021, when COVID-19 vaccines were newly introduced and many persons had not been exposed to the virus, reporting rate (defined as the number of adverse reports submitted to VAERS divided by the estimated number of vaccine doses administered per time interval) for persons 18–64 y was 14.92 cases/million doses. 79 During the subsequent years 2022–2023, the reporting rate for that same age group declined to 8.0 cases/million doses. 80 The risk of post-vaccination myocarditis has been evaluated according to vaccine dose and has declined with booster doses (compared with the two-dose primary series). 81 Review of VAERS reports for booster doses of mRNA-1273 (September 22, 2021–February 6, 2022) indicated that the reporting rate for myocarditis among adults was the highest in males aged 18–24 y, declining from 56.3 reports/million doses administered after second dose to 8.7 reports/million doses administered after third dose. 82 , 83

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: As is true for many vaccine-associated rare AEs, the immunopathogenesis for myocarditis following COVID-19 vaccination remains unknown. 84 It is clear that the demographic characteristics are similar to those of viral myocarditis, with risk rising with adolescence and predominantly in males, suggesting a role of testosterone-mediated immune modulation as a contributing factor. 85 Some early observational studies suggested that a greater antigen dose was a risk factor for vaccine-associated myocarditis. In one, rates of myocarditis among males 18–24 y, occurring within 7 d of the second dose of the primary series, were nominally lower for BNT162b2 (52.4 cases/million doses) than for mRNA-1273 (56.3 cases/million doses). 86 A shorter dosing interval for the primary series has been associated with greater risk, especially among younger males. The original EUAs for the vaccines called for a 21-d interval between first and second doses for BNT162b2 and 28 d for mRNA-1273. In a population based study conducted in Ontario, Canada (December 2020–September 2021, an increased risk of myocarditis was identified among 18–24 y old male mRNA-1273 recipients who received their second primary series dose 30 or fewer days after first dose compared with those receiving the second dose more than 30 d following first dose (83.9 cases/million doses [95% CI, 47.0–138.4 cases/million doses] vs 16.2 cases/million doses [95% CI, 10.2–24.6 cases/million doses], respectively). 87 Combined with nonclinical findings, this observation may suggest that shorter prime-boost dosing intervals are associated with a more pro-inflammatory response (that may in turn be a risk factor for myocarditis). 88 The risk for post-vaccine myocarditis is not restricted to mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccines; an elevated risk has also been observed with the adjuvanted protein-based vaccine, NVX-COV2373. 89 Independent of the complexity of understanding the cause(s) of COVID-19 vaccine-associated myocarditis, 84 it is important to recognize that the risks for and severity of myocarditis and pericarditis are greater due to COVID-19 disease than for myocarditis following vaccination. 84

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: In the setting of vaccination, syncope (fainting) is considered an anxiety-related event resulting in an acute vasovagal reaction, which may occur on account of the experience of vaccination (e.g. fear of receiving an injection) rather than as an immune response to the vaccine. 90 Circumstances such as a mass vaccine campaign may catalyze this type of response. 91 In April 2021, the CDC received reports of clusters of syncope cases at mass vaccination sites for the Ad26.COV2.S vaccine. Concerns for these anxiety-related vasovagal reactions in the setting of pandemic response were sufficient to issue a warning for the risk of syncope and were added to the product label for mRNA-1273 (it had already been present in the label for BNT162b2 and has since been added as well to NVX-CoV2373). The common verbiage notes that syncope could occur in association with “injectable vaccines” (i.e. not with any specific vaccine). 92 , 93

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: As a precautionary measure, vaccine development typically excludes certain groups from the phase III studies leading to initial product licensure. These groups, such as those who are pregnant or immunocompromised, may subsequently be evaluated through dedicated studies and/or pharmacovigilance activities.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Although people who were pregnant, planning to become pregnant, or breastfeeding were excluded from enrolling in mRNA-1273 clinical trials for EUA, a few incidental pregnancy cases (n = 13, 6 vaccine; 7 placebo) occurred in the phase III study. No adverse outcomes in pregnancy were reported in that study.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Early in 2020, pregnancy had been identified as a risk factor for severe COVID-19 outcomes, including the need for ICU admission and mechanical ventilation, 94 and was later found to be a risk factor for death from COVID-19. 95 COVID-19 was also identified as a risk factor for pre-term birth and adverse neonatal outcomes. 96 , 97 Given these considerations, and that nonclinical reproductive toxicity studies had not identified concerns, the American College of Obstetrics and Gynecology (ACOG), 98 shortly followed by the ACIP, recognized pregnancy as a high-risk medical condition for COVID-19, advising that those who were pregnant or breastfeeding could choose to be vaccinated. 99 Following introduction of the mRNA vaccines, the new CDC V-safe Pregnancy Registry provided nearly real-time data on pregnancy outcomes among pregnant women who had enrolled. Their initial assessment of rates of spontaneous abortion, stillbirth, term birth, and congenital anomalies found all of these to be within expected US background rates (among 3,958 participants). 100 Based upon the risk posed by COVID-19 in pregnancy and accruing information regarding vaccine safety in this group, in April 2021 the CDC recommended that pregnant women be vaccinated. Large studies conducted at this time 101 , 102 affirmed the initial findings from V-safe regarding safety from adverse pregnancy outcomes and have been further affirmed by more recent studies. 103 , 104 An observational retrospective study of 46,079 pregnant women (Dec 15, 2020–July 22, 2021) drawing data from 8 health care organizations participating in the Vaccine Safety Datalink women found that compared with contemporaneous pregnant women who did not receive an mRNA-COVID vaccine, there was no increased risk for small for gestational age (adjusted hazard ratio [aHR] 0.95; 95% CI 0.87–1.03) or preterm births (aHR 0.91; 95% CI 0.82–1.01). The findings applied to women who received one or more vaccine doses during the second and third trimesters (the sample size was inadequate to characterize the effect of exposure during the first trimester vaccination). 105 Maternal vaccination has also been found to be protective against COVID-19 hospitalization (including ICU admission/mechanical ventilation) in infants aged <6 months. 106

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: COVID-19 mRNA vaccines have also been evaluated in lactation. The design of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines results in rapid degradation of the LNP at the injection site and nearby lymph nodes, with minimal or no detectable mRNA in breast milk. 107 , 108 mRNA is present in nearly all consumed food and is rapidly degraded by RNAases. 109 Studies evaluating vaccine mRNA in human breast milk have either not detected it or identified it sporadically and transiently (less than 48 hours after vaccination) in trace amounts. 107 , 108 To date, post-licensure studies have not found lactation to be a risk factor for adverse infant outcomes in COVID-19 vaccinees. Since the original EUA, major public health agencies, including CDC and the WHO, have suggested vaccination for lactating women (and have subsequently strengthened their recommendations). Following vaccination, SARS-CoV-2 specific IgG and IgA antibodies have been shown to pass into breast milk, resist degradation, correlate with maternal levels, and have the potential to provide additional protection to the nursing infant. 110 , 111

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Recognizing that COVID-19 posed a serious risk to all age groups, early clinical research evaluating mRNA-1273 in younger adolescents was initiated in December 2020. An age de-escalation strategy was employed, successively enrolling younger age groups (12–17 y, 112 6–11 y, 113 6 months–5 y 114 ) only after safety had been characterized in the preceding older group, so that immune response and safety could be evaluated while safeguarding more vulnerable (younger) children. In this manner, the studies leading to the EUA (June 2022) ultimately selected doses for the primary COVID-19 vaccine series of 100, 50, and 25 µg in these age groups, respectively, each with a 28-d dosing interval.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: These clinical trials (with total sample sizes of 3,732 participants randomized 2:1, 4,016 randomized 3:1, and 4,802 randomized 3:1 [mRNA-1273 vs placebo], respectively) demonstrated an overall safety profile of the vaccine in the pediatric population that was largely similar to that previously observed in persons aged ≥18 y, although the proportions of local and systemic reactogenicity cases varied somewhat by age. 112–114 In older children, local reactogenicity (especially injection site pain), fatigue, and headache were more common. 112 , 113 In comparison, among those aged 6 months–5 y, age-appropriate systemic findings, including irritability, crying, sleepiness, and appetite loss were more common. 114 Symptom onset for most of these occurred within 1–2 d, with a median duration of 2–3 d. Grade 3 events were rare. No related cases of death or multi-inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C) were observed. 112–114

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: In the open-label phase of the trial in 12–17-y-olds, one 14-y-old male was considered to have probable myocarditis, with onset 1 d after second dose, resolution of symptoms 8 d later, and was without sequelae 28 d following event onset. 115 Several cases of fever ≥40°C were reported in the 6 months–5 y group. Vaccine safety was tracked promptly following the EUA (June 2022) for children aged 6 months and older, following the respective approaches that had been established by Moderna, the FDA, and CDC with the EUA for adults. For the period June–August 2022, the data collected through VAERS and V-safe were reviewed in the Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report (MMWR). 116 VAERS reports indicated that for children aged 6 months–2 y, the major reported systemic reactions for the mRNA vaccines were irritability and crying (occurring in approximately one-half of children). 116 For children 3 y and older, systemic reactions were less dominant while local reactions were more common. V-safe was used to proactively monitor infant experience (through a message-based smart phone reported by parents/caregivers) along with augmented data collected in VAERS and was especially informative for reactogenicity. Overall, the MMWR report concluded that reactogenicity and safety findings were similar to those previously reported for the clinical trials leading up to the EUA for mRNA-1273.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Pediatric febrile seizures are convulsions occurring in young children, most often aged 6 months–5 y, that typically occur in association with a viral infection and less commonly in association with certain vaccines. Among viral etiologies, respiratory viruses that have winter peaks in incidence, including Influenza A and B, respiratory syncytial virus, and adenovirus, as well as SARS-CoV2, have been associated with febrile seizures. These seizures are common (2–5% incidence in young children) usually brief, self-limited and benign, typically do not reflect underlying organic neurologic illness, and are generally isolated incidents and free from sequelae. 117 Febrile seizures have been reported as the most common neurological complication of COVID-19. In a cross-sectional multi-center study evaluating outcomes in 15,137 children with COVID-19-associated hospitalization, febrile seizures represented 38.9% of all neurological complications (n = 582; or 3.8%). 118 In addition to viral etiologies, several vaccines, including those targeting influenza, measles/mumps/rubella, diphtheria/tetanus/pertussis (acellular), polio (inactivated), and Haemophilus influenzae b , have been associated with an approximately 3–5 fold increased risk of febrile seizure compared with control population, typically within 0–12 d following vaccination. 117

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: An FDA/BEST analysis evaluated 21 prespecified outcomes among children aged 6 months–17 y who exclusively received either one of the ancestral monovalent mRNA COVID-19 vaccines (BNT162b2: n = 3,920,563; mRNA-1273: n = 176,427) or NVX-CoV2373 (n = 53) through early 2023. The time frame represented the first months of pediatric mRNA-1273 availability for those under 16 y of age; BNT162b2 had been approved for use in persons 16 and older in December 2020. 119 The authors identified a signal for seizure among children aged 2–5 y who had received an mRNA-based vaccine. The finding was further evaluated by sensitivity analysis, indicating a statistically significant signal specifically for febrile seizure, and only for mRNA-1273 recipients, when compared with one of two reference time periods (2019) that were used to provide background rates for febrile seizures. The authors suggested the finding be investigated through additional studies. A subsequent self-controlled case series assessment, using the same three FDA/BEST commercial insurance data sources, 120 indicated an elevated risk for febrile seizure, among 2–4-y-old recipients of mRNA-1273 within 1 d after vaccination. The risk difference (compared with that for d 8–63 following vaccination) equated to approximately 3 cases/100,000 doses (95% CI–0.31, 6.75). While the finding has not been corroborated by other sources, the potential for febrile seizures for children aged 2–5 y has been recognized in post-authorization safety surveillance communication (August 2024). COVID-19 is a risk factor for febrile seizures. Among children aged 6 months to <5 y, it has been estimated that there are approximately 190 cases/100,000 SARS-CoV-2 infections, greatly exceeding that associated with vaccination. 121

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: A range of disorders may result in immunocompromise, including: (1) inherited/primary immune conditions; (2) infections affecting immune response (e.g. HIV/AIDs); (3) acquired diseases, such as malignancies, diabetes mellitus, or autoimmune diseases; or (4) medical treatments that directly affect the immune system (e.g. the use of tumor necrosis factor-alpha treatment in managing Crohn’s disease). Such conditions may affect different components of immune response.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: As of December 2020 (when VRBPAC considered the EUA), safety data for mRNA-1273 on these populations had been restricted to persons with HIV/AIDS who were considered stable on treatment, and findings were similar to those of the general trial population. 36 Based on concern for the risk of severe COVID-19 in these populations, the mRNA COVID-19 vaccines were recommended for use in a broad range of immunocompromised individuals. 99 The primary concern for mRNA COVID-19 vaccines in immunocompromised persons was whether they would be sufficiently immunogenic, as suggested by suboptimal immune response after a two dose primary series. 122 To determine whether a 100-µg booster dose of mRNA-1273 might bolster the immune response, a trial was conducted in solid organ transplant recipients who had been previously vaccinated with that vaccine. Results demonstrated improved immunogenicity compared with the two-dose series, leading to a recommendation for a three-dose primary series in certain immunocompromised persons. 123 Compared with the Moderna phase III (COVE) trial participants, a lower proportion of subjects experienced reactogenic events (e.g. 77% experiencing injection site reactions [all grade 1–2] compared with 91.6% of COVE trials participants [including 9.1% grade 3 and no grade 4]). 124

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Although there may be specific safety considerations for different immunocompromised populations, the same general pattern has been found to be consistent in all these groups compared with the general population: local and systemic reactogenicity, without the identification and confirmation of additional safety concerns unique to these groups. Within the solid organ transplant population, a systematic review of mRNA vaccine experience did not find evidence for increased risk of transplant organ rejection. 125 In persons living with HIV/AIDS, a study of 510 persons (97% on antiretroviral therapy) who received COVID-19 mRNA vaccines was evaluated prior to and 1 month following vaccination. Vaccinees did not experience a decline in CD4+ T-cell count or an increase in viral load; in fact, there may have been a modest improvement in these parameters. 126 Among persons with a rheumatic/musculoskeletal disease, a meta-analysis that included 100 studies found the relative risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection to be 52% higher than the general population, with the risk of death 74% higher than the general population. 127 A retrospective cohort study conducted during the Omicron wave evaluated 2061 patients with a systemic autoimmune rheumatic disease and COVID-19 infection, and characterized the risk for hospitalization in older patients (adjusted odds ratio [aOR] 1.05/y (95% CI 1.04–1.07); African-Americans (aOR 4.15 [compared with White race], 95% CI 2.30–7.51), CD20 inhibitor therapy (aOR 2.22 [compared to antimalarial monotherapy], 95% CI 1.02–4.81), and glucocorticoid treatment (aOR 2.07 [compared with no glucocorticoid use], 95% CI 1.41–3.04). 128 COVID-19 vaccination has been shown to reduce the risk of COVID-19 related hospitalization in persons with systemic autoimmune rheumatic diseases (SARD). Compared with those who had not been vaccinated within the prior 12 months, SARD patients who had received a COVID-19 vaccine within <3 months and 3–6 months had a decreased risk of COVID-19 related hospitalization (aOR 0.41, 95% CI 0.22–0.75 and aOR 0.38, 95% CI 0.20–0.72, respectively). 128 A Korean population-level cohort study of more than 9 million individuals evaluated the safety of mRNA COVID-19 vaccines in persons with any of 16 autoimmune diseases and found that (compared with historical pre-pandemic controls) after 1 y of follow-up and receipt of at least one mRNA-1273 dose, there was no increased risk for disease flares; after a third dose, modest elevations were seen in a combined analysis of the two mRNA vaccines (adjusted hazard ratios for rheumatoid arthritis [1.14, 99% CI 1.08–1.21], alopecia areata [1.12, 1.05–1.19], psoriasis [1.16, 1.06–1.27]), though the authors noted that the effect size was small and needed to be considered in the setting of overall positive benefit. 129 A similarly designed study, also conducted in Korea, did not find elevated risk for incident autoimmune disease following receipt of at least one dose of an mRNA-COVID-19 vaccine, regardless of age, sex, or type of mRNA vaccine received. 130

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Adverse events of special interest are pre-specified AEs that, based on scientific evidence and biological plausibility, are of special interest in connection with a specific vaccine or vaccine platform, and which therefore merit focused and enhanced monitoring through active and/or passive surveillance. Specifying an AE as an AESI does not presume that a causal association has been established for a vaccine under development, only that it merits additional attention. 131 Indeed, the list of AESIs published by SPEAC 44 in advance of the introduction of any COVID-19 vaccines was intended to support a harmonized approach to the identification and evaluation of AEs that might be observed in the course of COVID-19 vaccine development, and included AESIs with: (1) established association with immunization (multiple vaccines); (2) prior association with vaccine/adjuvant/platform relevant for ≥1 candidate vaccines; (3) theoretical concerns based on immunopathogenesis, wild-type disease, or animal model; and (4) additions made based on safety signals identified regulatory authorities and required for inclusion in product information ( Table 1 ). Beyond clinical development, the list has been particularly useful for assessing specified AEs that are sufficiently uncommon to have been detected in the phase III vaccine trials (the phase III trials for each mRNA-based vaccine enrolled >30,000 participants), the most notable example being myocarditis.

metadata.title: From discovery through emergency use to the present: Safety evaluation of the COVID-19 mRNA-1273 (Moderna) vaccine.

text: Observational studies, often conducted within large databases coordinated by managed care organizations or insurers, have been useful to identify and characterize AESIs. Several studies have relied upon the Vaccine Safety Datalink (VSD), a research collaboration coordinated by the CDC and composed of 8 participating large health systems having sophisticated analytical capabilities. The VSD has played a central role in vaccine safety assessment since the 1990s. In the pandemic, the VSD provided early findings for COVID-19 mRNA vaccine safety after the initial EUA for adults and then for vaccine safety in children aged 6 months–5 y following EUA. 52 , 132 The former study characterized safety among 6.2 million persons who received 11.8 million mRNA vaccine doses, finding no meaningfully elevated risks for any of 23 AESIs within 1–21 d following vaccination (compared with a similar group 22–42 d after vaccination).
